title: Oil Prices Go Up After Trouble in a Waterway
summary: Oil prices went up again because of a problem in the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway in the Persian Gulf that is very important for moving oil around the world. Iran and the U.S. are having a standoff, which means they are not agreeing, and this stopped tankers from using the strait. When trading started on Sunday, the price of U.S. crude oil rose 6.4% to $87.88 per barrel, and Brent crude, which is used internationally, went up 6.5% to $96.25 per barrel. This happened after Iran said it would reopen the strait on Friday, causing prices to drop, but then changed its mind on Saturday after President Trump said the U.S. Navy would keep blocking Iranian ports. The conflict has been going on for eight weeks and is causing a big energy crisis, making gas prices high all over the world, especially in Asia and Europe. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said gas prices might not go below $3 a gallon until next year, but they have likely peaked and will start going down. Oil prices have changed a lot since the conflict started, from about $70 per barrel to over $119 at times.

a narrow waterway in the Persian Gulf where many oil tankers pass through

a big problem where there is not enough energy, like oil, for people to use

options: A standoff in the Strait of Hormuz
questions.correct_answer: A standoff in the Strait of Hormuz

How much did U.S. crude oil price increase?
Who said gas prices might not go below $3 a gallon until next year?

options: They import much oil from the Middle East
They are far from the strait
questions.correct_answer: They import much oil from the Middle East

background_read: Oil is a natural resource found underground and used to make gasoline for cars, diesel for trucks, and jet fuel for airplanes. The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow passage in the Persian Gulf, and many oil tankers sail through it to deliver oil to countries around the world. When there is trouble in this area, like disagreements between countries, it can stop the oil from moving, causing prices to go up because there is less oil available. This affects everyone because higher oil prices mean higher costs for gas and other things we use every day.
Article_Structure: WHO: Iran and the U.S. are involved in a standoff, and Energy Secretary Chris Wright commented on gas prices.
WHAT: Oil prices spiked due to a standoff in the Strait of Hormuz, stopping tankers and causing an energy crisis.
WHERE: The Strait of Hormuz in the Persian Gulf, affecting global markets, especially in Asia and Europe.
WHY: The standoff disrupted oil supplies, leading to higher prices and impacting consumers worldwide.

perspectives.description: The standoff causes oil prices to rise, making gas and other fuels more expensive for people and businesses.
